fr.name: Accélérer la restauration des prairies sous-marines dans la région méditerranéenne grâce à des solutions innovantes basées sur les services écosystémiques
fr.description:
Les prairies sous-marines recouvrent 1,2 million d'hectares des côtes de la zone Euro-MED. Des prairies saines fournissent des Services Écosystémiques (SÉ), estimés à >1 trillion€, essentiels aux sociétés et aux économies et stockant 18% du carbone bleu de la planète. Cependant, les herbiers marins restent l'un des écosystèmes les plus négligés de la zone Euro-MED et près de 7% sont détruits chaque année à cause de la pisciculture, du chalutage de fond, de l'ancrage, de l'érosion côtière et du ruissellement des nutriments: cette inaction a un coût.

Afin d’accélerer la restauration des herbiers marins, ARTEMIS intègre l’ensemble des dimensions sociétales et monétaires de ces écosystèmes, au travers de nouvelles méthodes techniques (protocole de restauration) et financières (premier système de Paiement pour les Services Écosystémiques - PSÉ) associées aux investissement et politiques des acteurs publics et privés.

ARTEMIS déploie 4 pilotes transnationaux incluant toutes les étapes de la restauration (de la transplantation dans les zones dégradées à l'amélioration des zones protégées) et tous les niveaux de politique (de l'absence de politiques de restauration aux décrets de protection), pour les autorités publiques, centres de recherche, ONG, entreprises, offices de tourisme, entraînant:


1.	La restauration de >2 hectares d'herbiers marins avec un rendement de 10x l'ÉS et un financement de 2 millions d'euros obtenus via des accords de PSÉ.


2.	Des résultats transnationaux; plan d'action Euro-Med et carte des opportunités de restauration, feuille de route pour l'intégration des SÉ en entreprises, outils de restauration, cadre d’évaluation des actions, guide pour les PSÉ.

10 partenaires de 5 pays assurent une expertise multidisciplinaire (économie, écologie, politique, engagement des parties prenantes, innovation) pour réaliser des projets pilotes, un apprentissage collaboratif et un développement de politiques, transférables à travers la zone Euro-MED.

fr.actual_achievements:
Period 1 was busy for the ARTEMIS project. The project set up, kick off and administrative procedures kept the partnership busy throughout the first six months of the project. The Partnership Agreement and the Subsidy Contract were both signed in May 2024. In terms of coordination, the project’s internal documentation, including work plans and task lists, was organized and monitored. Strategic planning efforts focused on refining project goals, ensuring all tasks aligned with ARTEMIS’ objectives, and fostering collaboration with partners and stakeholders. 

Project activities:

- Peer Learning Programme (A 1.1 / A 2.1 / A 3.1 / A 4.1): Bax designed an initial framework for the project's peer learning programme and collected partners' extensive input via a design thinking workshop delivered during the ARTEMIS partner meeting held in Marseille. Following this, the Bax team has been working on digesting the feedback received and conceptualising a first round of workshop ideas to be launched in period 2, in close coordination with the project's amplification efforts and identified stakeholders.

- WP1 implementation advanced well during Period 1. In Act 1.2, HCMR and ISPRA provided scientific knowledge to select adequate methodologies for building a baseline of the seagrass Ecosystem Services (ES) at each pilot site and to collect relevant data from the Greek pilot site. Regarding the selection of methodologies for the ecosystem service assessment, blue carbon and biodiversity were selected by the PP as the two main ES to be evaluated at the pilot sites. HCMR and ISPRA provided to the other PP the relevant literature, detailed methodologies, and personal experience on how to evaluate these services. For biodiversity estimation, the EBQI index, an ecosystem-based approach to assess the status of Posidonia oceanica seagrass meadows as outlined by Personnic et al. 2014, was suggested, with the addition of fixed transects or plots to obtain more detailed information on species recording. As for Activity 1.3, during the meeting in Marseille, ISPRA proposed a restoration strategy from the LIFE SEPOSSO project for common methodologies and facilitated the discussion and exchanges, providing general considerations and criteria to select the best approaches for the pilot sites. In this regard, a work schedule was proposed in order to acquire useful information about the pilot sites, directing the future restoration activities related to planning, implementing and monitoring activities.

WP2 implementation advanced well during Period 1. Act 2.2 includes stakeholder mappings that were carried out to: 
Identify potential companies interested in PES. Current scope – Greece.  
Identify potential knowledge partners with focus on seagrass restoration 
Identify potential initiatives focused on seagrass restoration and blue carbon 

Ecoacsa reviewed and contributed to the documentation and proposals shared by partners, regarding on how to link information from the Nature Capital Account (WP2) into the design of the Posidonia Payment for Ecosystem Services (PES) scheme/Market Based Instrument (MBI) and particularly how to link such information with the final outcome; being the blueprint (WP3). Ecoacsa is also working towards the adaptation of the Nature Value Tool (NVT) for seascapes, particularly to aid with the biophysical and economic quantification of seagrass ecosystem services (WP2 – A2.3).

- WP3 implementation advanced well during Period 1. The Peer Learning Programme module (A.3.1) started with a concept note shared by Bax to the partnership and A.3.2 started, Ecoacsa has coordinated internally and with Denkstatt EY a state-of-the art report on market-based instruments for seagrass restoration in the Mediterranean (defining content, structure and deadlines). 

- WP4 implementation advanced well during Period 1.
Act 4.1 started as highlighted above. 
Act 4.2 started, Preliminary outline of a concept note for Policy Outreach and Advocacy discussed between Plan Bleu and The Green Tank. 
Act 4.4 MEDSEA produced a Communication and Dissemination strategy which is available to the PP and the JS. in Basecamp. This was a major milestone achieved during this reporting period.
Act 4.5 Denkstatt as Carbon Footprint Manager in the project, provided guidelines to partners on the data required and arranged a Q&A session on how to input data to the Interreg carbon footprint calculator.
A 4.6 Project Amplification: Bax has designed the project's amplification strategy. This initial concept was validated with partners and the C4N project during the face to face meeting held in Marseille.

The big milestones across WP1-4 are the launch of the Peer Learning Programme (A 1.1 / A 2.1 / A 3.1 / A 4.1) and the production of initial concept notes for workshops to be organised in Period 2 as well as the organisation of the first Partner meeting in Marseille in May 2024.
The project has made significant progress during the second period and has reached important milestones. ARTEMIS has moved from the launch phase to the implementation phase with positive progress across all WPs, bringing the team closer to the expected results and stated ambition. 

Below we highlight the main milestones that ARTEMIS has reached: 

WP1:
The big milestones of WP1 during this reporting period was establishing a common understanding of key terminology, particularly baseline, pristine state, and reference value as well as upskilling the project pilot partner teams on the selected methodologies (Blue Carbon assessment and Biodiversity through EBQI Index) for ecosystem services baseline assessment. 

WP2: 
The big milestone of WP2 was the significant progress made on stakeholder engagement, especially as the Stakeholder Mapping methodology was elaborated in detail by the Green Tank (A2.2). leading to a template and guidelines suited to the ARTEMIS project’s objectives. The template was thoroughly populated with stakeholders relevant to the Greek pilot site and was presented initially to WP leaders and then all ARTEMIS partners for feedback. Once input was received and considered, the Stakeholder Table and Power Grid were finalized and shared with all ARTEMIS partners along with clear guidelines (November 2024), so that project partners working on the pilot sites could populate them with relevant data. The adoption of a common methodology led to a unified manner to identify key stakeholders across all pilot sites adding to the project’s methodological robustness. The Stakeholder Table and Grids were completed for the pilot sites in Crete, Monfalcone and Minorca by the end of 2024, while the one for Sardinia is being completed. It is envisioned that the Stakeholder Tables and Grids will be working documents to be updated as the project progresses and used across the various project actions. The completion of the Stakeholder mapping sets the foundation of many other activities of the project. In addition, significant progress was made on stakeholder engagement. This step involved the design of a questionnaire aimed at facilitating interviews and meetings that would elucidate the level of knowledge and perception of selected stakeholders on issues relevant to ARTEMIS. The questionnaire was thus structured on three axes: a) Knowledge of and on Posidonia Oceanica and restoration actions, b) Governance of Posidonia Oceanica conservation and restoration and c) Perceptions on Payment for Ecosystem Services. This questionnaire and a discussion guide with key points, was shared with all project partners to be used as an example across ARTEMIS pilot sites. 

WP3: 
Significant achievements were made in WP3 with the delivery of the State of the Art Report on Market-Based Instruments for Seagrass Restoration. 

WP4:
In terms of policy work, together with The Green Tank, a preliminary outline of a concept note for Policy Outreach and Advocacy was prepared to guide the policy relevant actions. In addition, the transnational policy analysis, aimed to document the global, EU and regional policy and legal provisions for the restoration of seagrass restoration ahead of the elaboration of specific policy recommendations (A4.2), was initiated. For the purposes of this analysis, mapping of existing policy and legal frameworks and bibliographical research relevant to ARTEMIS were undertaken. In addition, to supporting the transnational policy analysis, the mapping and research helped identify points on which all pilot sites need to focus, as they engage with stakeholders. These were then integrated in the questionnaire elaborated. At the same time, the stakeholder interviews undertaken by The Green Tank have already provided valuable information that will complement the research and analysis undertaken, and, as such, will enrich its content, conclusions and recommendations.Communication and dissemination actions took place within the project, coordinated by MEDSEA. Many articles and social media posts were produced, positioning the project and increasing its visibility. One important milestone was the Press Workshop organised by MEDSEA to develop a press release plan for the next semester. In terms of amplification activities, many partners participated in the second annual dialogue on 28th 29th November, in Malaga. Plan Bleu organized a roundtable where ARTEMIS was presented, and connection with other thematic projects were made. Discussions took place with partners on the organisation of an ARTEMIS workshop during the European Seagrass Restoration Alliance conference in April 2025. Finally, Plan Bleu participated in regular meetings and joined the Mediterranean Posidonia Network Taskforce. Plan Bleu will participate in the MPN seminar in May 2025 in Tunisia, and will present ARTEMIS work which will feed into a regional strategy for the conservation and restoration of seagrass meadows in the Mediterranean.

en.name: Accelerating the Restoration of Seagrass Meadows in the Mediterranean area through Innovative ecosystem-service based Solutions
en.description:
Seagrass meadows cover 1.2M hectares along Euro-MED coastlines. Healthy meadows deliver critical ecosystem services (ES) to societies and economies, storing 18% of the world’s blue carbon, and provide >1€ trillion in coastal protection, recreational opportunities for tourism or nursery services to commercial fisheries (UN, 2022). However, seagrasses are still one of the most overlooked ecosystems in the Euro-MED area and close to 7% of meadows are lost annually due to pressures like fish farming, bottom trawling, anchoring, coastal erosion and nutrient runoff: the cost of inaction.

To accelerate the restoration of seagrass meadows in the Euro-MED area, ARTEMIS integrates the societal and monetary values of seagrass ES into technical (state-of-the art restoration protocols), financial (first-of-their-kind Payment for Ecosystem Services schemes (PES) targeting multiple ES) and relevant public and private policies and investments.

We implement 4 transnational pilots covering all restoration stages (from transplantation in degraded areas to the enhancement of protected ones) and levels of policy (from absence of restoration policies to protection Decrees), leading to the following outputs for restoration and blue economy actors: public authorities, research centres, NGOs, companies, tourism agencies:



1. >2 hectares of seagrass meadows restored with 10x returns in ES and €2M additional funding secured via PES agreements.


2. Transnational outputs: i) Euro-Med action plan and opportunity map for seagrass restoration, ii) roadmap for corporate integration of seagrass ES, iii) technical seagrass restoration toolbox, iv) actionable framework for seagrass valuation and storytelling, v) ready-to-use guidelines for seagrass PES.

10 project partners from 5 countries provide multidisciplinary expertise (economics, ecology, policy, stakeholder engagement, innovation) to deliver joint pilots, peer learning and policy development, replicable across the Euro-MED area.
en.expected_achievements:
Implement seagrass restoration pilots as a testing ground for state-of-the art restoration techniques and gathering of relevant ecosystem service data for the design of PES schemes and connection to policy.
Generate awareness on the value of meadows to build a case for public and private investment in seagrass restoration by conducting seagrass ecosystem service valuations (qualitative and quantitative) and through stakeholder engagement. 
Test, validate and scale PES as a mechanism to accelerate financial flows from public and private bodies towards seagrass restoration projects.

Effectively integrate the value of seagrass ecosystem services into relevant public and private governance frameworks in the Euro-MED area and neighbouring countries in the southern Mediterranean basin.

en.name: Testing novel environmental quality measures in and around Euro-MED ports
en.description:
TREASURE’s overall objective is to improve environmental quality in and around Mediterranean port areas, by mitigating and reducing sediment and water pollution.

Human activities cause unprecedented environmental changes in coastal and marine ecosystems. Port areas become both the final accumulation site for chemicals from anthropic activities and a cause of pollution that is difficult to contain. This represents a significant environmental risk for marine biodiversity and surrounding areas and a risk to human health.

Within this overall challenge, TREASURE identifies 3 sub-challenges:
CHALLENGE 1: GOVERNANCE – need for cross-sector and cross-cutting governance frameworks to improve environmental quality in port areas.
CHALLENGE 2: MONITORING ENVIRONMENTAL QUALITY – need for a common and advanced monitoring framework, able to assess impact and pressures on port environments.
CHALLENGE 3: NOVEL TECHNIQUES –need to support commercialisation and roll-out of new techniques to remediate polluted sediments and water.

Our approach, capitalising on previous projects, merges 3 multi-disciplinary Action Lines to address the core challenges and produce the integrated TREASURE solution (key output):
ACTION LINE 1: Multi-stakeholder Supporting Ecosystem for the creation and management of territorial labs. 
ACTION LINE 2: New approaches to environmental assessment, through application of an integrated matrix of criteria and using the Lines of Evidence approach for qualitative evaluation.
ACTION LINE 3: coordinated sourcing and experimentation of novel techniques for pollution reduction in sediments and water, allowing for validation in industrially relevant conditions.

This integrated approach is novel, as is our proposal for multi-stakeholder labs, our Lines of Evidence approach and our focus on novel techniques.

Transnational cooperation is necessary to achieve our objectives, both due to the common challenges that we address and to the innovative approach that we propose.
en.expected_achievements:
To plan, implement and exchange on transnational testing of the Multi-stakeholder supporting ecosystem for better environmental quality in Mediterranean port areas. Change comes with consolidation of mini-territorial labs across TREASURE territories.
To create a common understanding of new approaches to environmental assessment in Mediterranean port areas, leading to creation of an Impact Evaluation and Hazard Management matrix that is tested at transnational level in TREASURE territories.
To source and undertake transnational testing of novel techniques for environmental remediation in Mediterranean port areas, within the mini-lab format, leading to solid transnational results about their cost-effectiveness / impact.
To consolidate the TREASURE Solution and ensure its long-term mainstreaming and transfer, encouraging better environmental quality in Mediterranean port areas.
en.actual_achievements:
In Period1 (P1), all WPs were launched with the following achievements:

On 05/03/24 a virtual meeting was held. WPs’ work plans for P1/2 were presented by WP leaders. To create multi-stakeholder governance ecosystems (WP1) a tool to gather preliminary input on territorial background on environmental challenges and stakeholders was shared. To introduce the new approach to environmental assessment (WP2), project GEREMIA was presented, together with a form to verify partners’ capability in environmental data sampling. The schedule for sourcing novel techniques for environmental remediation (WP3) was shared.

WP 1 - At Kick-off meeting (KO) (Livorno 03-04/04/24), TANIA project was presented to capitalise its knowledge on novel solutions for remediation. With an eye to the signature of territorial agreements for instating mini-labs in TREASURE, all partners presented territorial analyses.

In June, a template was shared to prepare the signature of  territorial cooperation protocols. 

All partners set up local stakeholder groups by end of P1.

WP 2 – At KO, the Lines of Evidence approach was deepened. All partners reported on stakeholders’ scientific knowledge, data available and data collection capability.

WP 3 – at KO, the questionnaire designed by PP6 to select environmental challenges was presented. Partners highlighted missing subjects to expand the proposed topics. In P2 environmental challenges will be addressed by a call for interest to catalogue novel remediation solutions.

In May the questionnaire was circulated. All partners, associates and a number of stakeholders answered to it. On 25/06/24, a virtual meeting was held to validate the results.

WP4 - PP9 elaborated the communication strategy and its tools, including: project poster, templates, social media, internal monitoring. Tools and strategy were presented at KO.

LP and PP6 attended all virtual meetings of Natural Heritage Mission, shared infos on TREASURE and joined the mission launch event (Rovinj 26-27/06/24).
In Period 1 and 2 (P1/2), TREASURE project launched its activities and progressed effectively in accordance to its work plan. Activities and exchange gradually increased, with the first trimester of Period 1 mainly dedicated to set up internal monitoring and coordination procedures, while technical implementation actually started since April 2024. 
Throughout P1/2, all partners provided sound contribution in addressing the 3 project challenges to environmental quality in and around Mediterranean port areas. The interregional cooperation demonstrated extremely valuable since the project start, with partners directly involved by the WP leaders in building a multi-stakeholder supporting environment for mini-labs (Action Line 1), deepening and testing new approaches to environmental assessment through the Lines of Evidence (LoEs) approach (Action Line 2), and sourcing and experimenting novel techniques for environmental remediation (Action Line 3). The interregional cooperation provided from start a significant added value, providing for the opportunity to compare different but coherent environmental challenges and to reach out a wide range of stakeholders gravitating around the project port areas.
WP1
Within the first work package, partners acknowledged a multi-stakeholder approach to lay the foundations to transnational environmental testing, capitalising on the experience of the Interreg Europe project TANIA, funded under MFF 2014-2020. Within each TREASURE port area, partners begun to map and build a local stakeholder ecosystem, with the aim to formalise a group of regional actors involved in the management of mini-territorial labs, around which testing activities will be implemented. By the end of P2, most TREASURE partners signed local agreements for running mini-labs within TREASURE scope, with a selected group of stakeholders. All partners are expected to finalise their agreements by P3.
WP2
Within the second work package, partners began to create a common understanding of a new approach to environmental assessment, capitalising on the results of GEREMIA project, funded under the Programme Italy-France Maritime 2014-2020. The LoEs approach, aims to integrate vast amounts of environmental data into complex monitoring scenarios. Such approach allows for the creation of an integrated environmental risk index, which will be tested by TREASURE partners. By the end of P2, all partners with relevant competence on their port areas selected a number of LoEs and related thresholds to monitor, feeding into an Impact Evaluation and Hazard Management matrix that is tested at transnational level in TREASURE.
WP3
Within the third work package, partners cooperated in elaborating, launching, and disseminating a call for interest aimed to source and select a number of innovative solutions for environmental remediation. In P1, partners identified a set of environmental challenges characterising their port areas, addressing which the call for interest was released in P2. A number of solutions submitted to the call are awarded building on their relevance for TREASURE port areas, and gathered into a catalogue for further testing within the mini-lab framework.
WP4
Within the fourth work package, partners defined a project Monitoring and Evaluation methodology to set the ground to project implementation and later transferability of results. Coordination among key project roles and partners was set up in P1, fostering exchange at project level. Exchange with regional and international stakeholders was facilitated also thanks to the coordination with the Natural Heritage Mission governance project. 
The above mentioned cooperation developed revolving around several virtual and two in-person meetings, in particular the Kick-off meeting in Livorno (03-04/04/2024) and the second meeting in Valencia (15-16/10/204), during which the partnership validated the exchange carried out through the internal documents and organised valuable study visits to the hosting porta areas.
In terms of concrete outputs, two deliverables were produced by the end of P2. WP2 generated D2.2.1TREASURE Environmental Impact evaluation and Hazard Management matrix, to provide the overall environmental quality assessment matrix developed at transnational level, using the LoEs approach. It gathers the initial indicators for a qualitative evaluation of different compartments (sediments / water / biota) of the port environments subject to environmental testing within WP2.
WP3 produced D3.1.1Open call for interest methodology, to present the methodology used to design and implement the call for interest, along which all the supporting documents i.e., call text, application form, evaluation criteria, selection process were shared to allow a fair and transparent selection of novel techniques for remediation.
The output indicator 4.4 was obtained at project start, counting 15 organisations, including partners and associates, cooperating across the border.
In Period 3 (P3), TREASURE partners cooperated to obtain the following achievements:

On 23/01/2025, during a final selection virtual meeting, all project partners validated the innovative solutions submitted to the TREASURE call for interest to improve environmental quality of port areas. 

On 18-19/02/2025, partners and stakeholders convened at meeting 03 in Durres (AL), during which data collection methods were discusses to apply environmental management matrixes and to plan data sampling in each territory (WP2), progress in solution testing activities was presented (WP3), an innovative solution workshop was organised on Water Management/Quality and Biodiversity preservation (WP3), synergies with ASAP and TRUEBLUE projects were deepened (WP4).

On 13/03/2025, a virtual meeting was held. WPs’ work plans for P3/4 were presented by all WP leaders, in order to coordinate the interregional exchange in the upcoming year.

On 26-27/06/2025, partners participated in meeting 04 in Bar (ME), during which an innovative solution workshop was organised on Air quality and Energy management in ports (WP3), an update on state of testing activities was presented (WP2), progress in solution testing activities was presented (WP3), the Governance Partner Report (WP1) and Mainstreaming Action Plan and Transferability Strategy (WP4) were discussed in depth and validated.

WP1 – Cooperation with local governance ecosystems continued to support implementation of testing activities in WP2/3, leading to tailor-made governance models and territorial mini-labs. In May, the LP shared a Governance Partner Report template to summarise local context analyses, including environmental challenges and port area stakeholders, to report on agreements signed for the implementation of TREASURE approach, and to present the local customization of the mini-lab concept. All partners continued work to finalise the signature of the local agreements with the stakeholders involved in the TREASURE mini-labs.

WP2 – A first phase was dedicated to the planning of environmental data sampling within each port, and the development of the dedicated web-based tool for data processing. All partners planned sampling activities and related tender procedures.  The demo web-based tool to integrate data was tested by partners to familiarise with it and gather feedback. 3 PPs (ISPRA, DPA, FV) performed the first samplings of environmental data to feed the common Impact Evaluation and Hazard Management Matrix.

WP 3 – 52 innovative solutions for improvement of environmental quality, out of 59 submitted to the TREASURE call for interest, were approved by the partnership. The awarded solutions were noticed and promoted through the TREASURE catalogue, published in June 2025. 2 workshops with solution providers were held during project meetings to present solutions and to network with project partners and port authorities, representing also an opportunity to better understand the challenges ports are facing and how to better adapt technologies to those. Study visits were organised after each workshop. Partners mapped the scope of the pilot testing activities and began related public procurements.

WP4 - LP and CEI joined all NHM physical/online initiatives, participated in Working Groups, and contributed to the Euro-MED Academy preparation. Partners monitored and evaluated TREASURE activities, assigning roles and responsibilities at project and port level. Partners agreed to launch online surveys for partners/stakeholders to receive feedback on the quality and accessibility of project activities. LP drafted an Action Plan template, to be filled in by partners, outlining how to integrate and follow up on project results in local practice, including an overview of local policies/strategies for the port area, a summary of key achievements, and a plan for exploiting results. CEI presented a Strategy for Transferability of Results, to promote project solutions and outcomes outside the consortium. A roadmap of initiatives for result transfer was defined, to be implemented until June 2026, including online (3) and in persons (2) workshops.

fr.name: Testing novel environmental quality measures in and around Euro-MED ports
fr.description:
L'objectif global de TREASURE est d'améliorer la qualité de l'environnement dans et autour des zones portuaires méditerranéennes, en atténuant et en réduisant la pollution des sédiments et de l'eau.
Les activités humaines provoquent des changements environnementaux sans précédent. Les zones portuaires deviennent à la fois le site d'accumulation finale des produits chimiques et une cause de pollution difficile à contenir. Cela représente un risque environnemental pour la biodiversité marine et les zones environnantes, ainsi qu'un risque pour la santé humaine.
Dans ce cadre, TREASURE identifie 3 sous-défis :
1 : GOUVERNANCE - nécessité de cadres de gouvernance intersectoriels et transversaux pour améliorer la qualité environnementale dans les zones portuaires.
2 : SURVEILLANCE - nécessité d'un cadre de monitorage commun et avancé, capable d'évaluer l'impact et les pressions sur les environnements portuaires.
3 : TECHNIQUES NOUVELLES - nécessité de soutenir la commercialisation et le déploiement de nouvelles techniques pour assainir les sédiments et les eaux pollués.
Notre approche, qui s'appuie sur des projets antérieurs, fusionne 3 lignes d'action pluridisciplinaires pour produire la solution intégrée TREASURE (résultat clé) :
1 : Ecosystème de soutien multi-acteurs pour la création et la gestion des laboratoires territoriaux.
2 : Nouvelles approches d'évaluation environnementale, par l'application d'une matrice intégrée de critères et l'utilisation de l'approche « Lines of Evidence » (LOE).
3 : Approvisionnement coordonné et expérimentation de nouvelles techniques de réduction de la pollution dans les sédiments et l'eau.
Cette approche intégrée est nouvelle, tout comme notre proposition de laboratoires multipartites, notre approche fondée sur les LOE et notre attention aux nouvelles techniques.
La coopération transnationale est nécessaire pour atteindre nos objectifs, tant en raison des défis communs que nous relevons, que de l'approche innovante que nous proposons.
fr.expected_achievements:
To plan, implement and exchange on transnational testing of the Multi-stakeholder supporting ecosystem for better environmental quality in Mediterranean port areas. Change comes with consolidation of mini-territorial labs across TREASURE territories.
To create a common understanding of new approaches to environmental assessment in Mediterranean port areas, leading to creation of an Impact Evaluation and Hazard Management matrix that is tested at transnational level in TREASURE territories.
To source and undertake transnational testing of novel techniques for environmental remediation in Mediterranean port areas, within the mini-lab format, leading to solid transnational results about their cost-effectiveness / impact.
To consolidate the TREASURE Solution and ensure its long-term mainstreaming and transfer, encouraging better environmental quality in Mediterranean port areas.

en.name: Strategy for the Sustainable Management of Mediterranean Forests
en.description:
The StrategyMedFor project has gathered 7 PPs in 6 countries of the Interreg Cooperation area (including 2 IPA countries) plus relevant APs from the forest EU & international scene, to support mission 2 of the programme to protect, restore & valorise the Natural environment & Heritage. This project addresses particularly to the Mediterranean Forest Ecosystem. 
Attending EU & international strategies & policies about forests, we tackle needs & gaps of knowledge on strategies & action plans for the sustainable management of the Mediterranean forests. For that reason, the main goal of the StrategyMedFor is the development of a Strategy for Sustainable Management of Mediterranean Forests (SSMMF) targeted to each forest type & adapted to foreseeable climate scenarios. The novelty of the work is the integration of new technologies & wide-Mediterranean data into a spatial context, combining geospatial data, remote sensing, GIS & climate modelling in the development of the Strategy. 
The change we envisage is that our SSMMF will be a reference document for the next 10 years in the EU & Mediterranean scene, by providing scientific-sound recommendations about how & which forests to protect, restore or manage into resilience. Specific tools are planned, such as thematic cartography about the status of the forests in real time, & the priority areas for action, to make sustainable management more efficient at regional, national & local scale. 
We commit to the implementation of some of the results of the StrategyMedFor project by engaging operational partners in key test areas in countries (Eastern Mediterranean) where we identified scarce investment in research &/or protection of Mediterranean forests, an to transfer knowledge & consolidate cross-country collaborations to the broad Mediterranean region (N-Africa & MiddleEast). 
In addition, our results will be amplified to the Interreg community via the TCP, IDP & other communication channels of the programme.
en.expected_achievements:
The objective of this WP is to develop a Strategy for the sustainable management of Mediterranean Forests and define a set of guidelines, criteria and indicators towards Mediterranean forests protection, restoration and resilience.

Develop geospatial data on the status of and impacts on forests and identify priority areas for local to regional actions to guide protection, restoration and management in support of the Strategy for Sustainable Management of Mediterranean Forests.

Activities to develop on 3 test pilot areas (BiH, GR and MK) (1) to validate SSMMF and maps, (2) design and implement specific action plans and (3) extension of IBP
Capitalization of knowledge & experiences delivered in WP1, WP2 & WP3 supporting HEV forest protection, restoration & management for resilience, by fostering knowledge reuse and multilateral dialogue in target areas and in the Mediterranean basin.
en.actual_achievements:
During the first reporting period of the StrategyMedFor project, each partner contributed significantly to advancing the project's objectives across different work packages.
ETC-UMA, as Lead Partner, played a key role in facilitating communication and coordination among all project partners. The LP made sure to agree with all partners and implement a major change in the Application form by exchanging one partner (DDIP ZDC) from Bosnia and Herzegovina with another (Faculty of Forestry - University of Sarajevo) as DDIP ZDC communicated their lack of capacities to remain a project partner.

During this period, ETC-UMA organised general and bilateral meetings to kick off the project activities. A pre-kickoff meeting was held in February 2024 (online) and the kickoff meeting in March in Malaga. Both meetings were crucial for setting the governance structure and establishing a work and communication flow. ETC-UMA also led the technical aspects of WP2 by reviewing existing geospatial data and initiating a data archive, collaborating with partners like Istituto Oikos, CNPF, and CIHEAM-MAICh to establish criteria for cartographic content. Additionally, ETC-UMA supported communication activities by contributing to the Communication Strategy, developing project brochures, and creating drafts for story maps for the project and the pilot sites.

Istituto Oikos participated in partnership meetings supporting activities in WP1, WP2, and WP4. At the kickoff meeting, they presented WP4 activities and organised the Annual Medforval Meeting in June 2024, introducing StrategyMedFor to the Medforval network. They also contributed to the understanding of High Ecological Value (HEV) forests for WP2 and launched a survey to prepare the structure of the MOOC under WP4. They prepared the groundwork for the dissemination of project knowledge through various online platforms.

AIFM leads the external communication activities. They developed the project's Communication Strategy and Action Plan drafts and are validating them with project partners in addition to templates for communication material. They developed the project's brochure, and launched the official project website and social media channels. AIFM followed up on the Carbon Footprint Calculator tool, reporting issues and preparing for a seminar on its usage. Their participation in project meetings and events, such as the Natural Heritage Mission meeting in Rovinj, underscored their commitment to effective communication and outreach. 

CIHEAM-MAICh focused on initiating WP1 and supported some WP2 discussions. Under WP1, they are overseeing the shaping of associated criteria and indicators for a visionary Strategy for the Sustainable Management of Mediterranean Forests (SSMMF). They supported an online introductory workshop with associated partners that took place in June 2024 to identify the main criteria important for the MEditerranean forests that the Strategy needs to include. CIHEAM-MAICh also reviewed existing geospatial data for use in thematic mapping for the case study of Samaria Park that they lead on.

PIGNP  contributed to the creation of a data archive (D2.1.1), which will serve as a tool for evaluating the status of Mediterranean forests, identifying impacts, and facilitating forest protection, restoration, or management actions. For WP1, PIGNP provided assistance in communication activities (A1.5) by contributing content for the Storymap and content template related to Galicica NP. This contribution was essential for fulfilling deliverable D1.5.1, the "Communication Protocol on Exchanging Information and Processing the Participation of Stakeholders," fostering by this cross-border cooperation for the sustainable management of Mediterranean forest ecosystems.

UNSA FoF leads WP3 effectively, coordinating activities related to the pilot sites as well as testing, validation, and implementation of project tools and methodologies. They completed necessary administrative tasks to join the project, established communication channels, and organised WP3 meetings focused on selecting pilot sites and planning future actions. UNSA FoF also participated in WP1 and WP2 activities, assisting in developing research questions and contributing to spatial data screening. Additionally, they supported communication efforts by creating up-to-date content for the project’s story maps and establishing social media profiles, further enhancing the project's visibility.

CNPF were late in joining the partnership activities, as the main expert that was expected to join StrtegyMedFor was on long medical leave. Some replacements were ensured by CNPF for the most essential meetings in period 1. This situation is expected to improve in period 2 with the incorporation of the CNPF expert assigned to the project.

The partnership has ensured a strong start to the StrategyMedFor project, with significant progress made in communication, technical preparation, and the initiation of key work packages.
During Period 2, online and in-person meetings were essential for advancing project activities, sharing progress, and engaging stakeholders. A key event was the Consultation Workshop in Crete (September 17–19), where project advancements were presented, and stakeholder feedback was gathered. Partners also visited one project case study, the Samaria National Park.  The visit was used as a demonstration exercise for the application of the IBP method in the field and to define priorities for restoration actions. Partners defined the work ahead to finalise communication strategy and action plan and confirmed the interest of using story maps and dashboards to communicate, through spatial data, project progressively.

In November, the project team participated in the 8th Mediterranean Forest Week (Nov 4–7, 2024), and led a session focusing on StrategyMedFor C&I. The partnership presented two posters on the insights on Forest Europe Criteria and Indicators and an approach used and initial results of the spatialisation of Forest cover indicator. On November 8, the 2nd Steering Committee meeting gathered all partner organizations to plan upcoming activities. A working group on criteria and indicators was formed, including members from WP1, WP2, and the University of Copenhagen. Discussions with EFIMED explored synergies with the RessAlliance project.

ETC-UMA, as LP, ensured smooth coordination, effective communication, and support for activities on criteria, indicators, and forest biodiversity. The SC meeting also helped resolving some administrative and budgetary questions that partners had, specifically PIGNP resolved the issue linked to the vehicle acquisition and vehicle issue and UNSA FoF clarified doubts about the budget linked to site level work.

Under WP2, the deliverable “D.2.1.1 Data Archive” was developed and made available via the Mediterranean Biodiversity Protection Knowledge Platform (MBPKP), with ongoing updates. Progress on the local-scale cartographic material was discussed with partners, focusing on forest areas and fragmentation. Pilot site managers played a crucial role in validating the selected model. Some initial exchanges took place discussing the biodiversity parameters to use to assess forest biodiversity and ecological status.

Istituto Oikos contributed to WP4, advancing the MOOC (A.4.1) and the Prespa-Ohrid Transboundary Management Plan (A.4.2). An anonymous questionnaire with 59 responses was launched, and collaboration with H2020 EU-Farmbook began. Discussions with key stakeholders took place for A.4.2, while feedback was provided on criteria and indicators. Oikos also validated maps and collected spatial data for WP2, reviewed the WP3 work plan, and participated in major project events.

CNPF has participated in the Consultation Workshop with a presentation of the IBP, followed by a demonstration during the field visit. CNPF also started working on the literature review for the adaptation of the IBP to the Mediterranean context and actively participated in the Working Group meetings on Criteria and Indicators.

As communication lead, AIFM worked on ensuring project visibility. The Communication Strategy and Action Plan were approved by partners in October 2024. Deliverables included multilingual posters and roll-up banners. A project presentation video is set for release on January 31, 2025. Social media engagement increased with five posts and two articles. AIFM joined the Chania workshop and organized collaboratively with partners the side event at the Mediterranean Forest Week in Barcelona.

CIHEAM-MAICh reviewed strategies (A.1.1), defined forest management criteria and indicators, hosted and organized, collaboratively with partners, the first in-person consultation workshop in September 2024 (A.1.2). In A.1.3, progress was done within the WG on C&I to select criteria for geodata and started forest mapping for protection and resilience. They also ensured “green event” practices for carbon footprint monitoring (A.1.4) and contributed to communication strategy validation (A.1.5).

PIGNP contributed to strategy development, criteria setting, and thematic information layers. PIGNP engaged in workshops, including a consultation in Greece and a Steering Committee meeting in Spain, presenting key insights on Galicica National Park. It collaborated with PrespaNet partners and informed stakeholders via the METT tool. Additionally, PIGNP submitted an annual report detailing 2024 activities to the Ministry of Environment of North Macedonia.

UNSA FoF coordinated pilot site activities in Bosnia, Greece, & NM. They prepared a draft WP3 WP, helped develop criteria for sustainable forest management, and validated geospatial data. UNSA FoF maintained strong communication with partners and stakeholders, actively participating in meetings, workshops, and 8MFW. They also supported carbon footprint reporting, communication strategy, and promotional materials, improving project visibility.
The StrategyMedFor project has made substantial progress during Period 3, consolidating its Mediterranean cooperation dimension and strengthening the technical, methodological, and communication foundations required to achieve its objectives. Under the coordination of the Lead Partner, several bilateral and joint meetings and one steering committee were organised during this reporting period to ensure consistency across work packages. These exchanges also facilitated the mobilisation of associated partners and target groups, who engaged actively through surveys, online sessions, and in-person workshops, thereby enriching the process with diverse perspectives and reinforcing its legitimacy.

Major progress was made within WPs 1 and 2, and WP3 kicked off and developed its roadmap during this reporting period. Within WP1, a draft SSMMF and a list of Criteria and indicators were developed including a policy brief, technical report on criteria and indicators (C&I), and a user guide for forest managers. Consultation workshops, both online and in Sarajevo, gathered stakeholders to validate the framework and refine supplementary indicators. Contributions from partners such as UNSA FoF, Istituto Oikos, and PIGNP ensured that the C&I were tested against practical realities, with feedback drawn directly from pilot sites and park-level management experiences.
Within WP2 a number of ancillary spatial data and indicators were tested, validated within the partnership, and produced at Mediterranean and local scales.

Significant progress was achieved in the development of climate scenarios and the production of spatial data layers on forest area, fragmentation, landscape mosaics, and high ecological value. These outputs were discussed and validated closely to partners, in terms of methodology validation, result testing, etc. These results are used to inform the strategy’s technical framework and to provide a knowledge base for regional and local purposes. The testing sites, with a special effort made by PIGNP, supported this effort by collecting additional data and by validating local geospatial datasets and preparing species lists (specially for Galicica National Park), adding site-specific expertise to the regional database.

WP3 kicked off in this reporting period, and several partners meetings were made to set up a roadmap for action and site level activities started in the 3 sites. A major milestone was the Index of Biodiversity Potential (IBP) training organised by the CNPF in Toulouse in April. The training on the use of IBP for partners and associated partners was designed to train the group on the IBP applicability. After the training, several partners applied the IBP method in several pre-identified plots with the guidance of the CNPF. During this period, field activities focusing on adapting the IBP definition to the Balkan region and testing it in Bosnia and Herzegovina were made through a collaborative effort between UNSA FoF and CNPF. 

Action plans for pilot sites in Bosnia and Herzegovina, Greece, and North Macedonia were prepared. The second regional consultation workshop took place in Sarajevo during which a field visit to the Dinaric Alps enabled direct validation of indicators and methodologies to develop spatial indicators. PIGNP began implementing its pilot site activities, preparing timelines for Grecian juniper conservation measures, and engaging in a transboundary planning process with partners from Albania and Greece. This collaboration is expected to result in a joint conservation plan for the Prespa/Ohrid region, demonstrating the project’s capacity to foster cross-border solutions for shared ecological challenges.

Istituto Oikos advanced Work Package 4 by coordinating the design of the project’s Massive Open Online Course (MOOC) on Mediterranean forest protection, restoration, and sustainable management. Structured in multiple interactive modules, the MOOC is being developed in close alignment with the Interreg Euro-MED Academy platform. Oikos started the preparations for the upcoming Prespa/Ohrid transboundary workshop, and contributed by supporting the spatialisation attempts of the High Ecological Value index.

On the communication front, AIFM ensured project visibility and stakeholder engagement through the production of promotional videos, news articles, and social media content, alongside the design of consultation documents and event facilitation tools. Its contributions included the production of a highlight video during the Sarajevo workshop and the development of a publication calendar to strategically disseminate project outputs.

Spending deviations this period were minor. AIFM and CIHEAM-MAICh had small changes due to activity timing and resource adjustments and PIGNP could do the purchase of the vehicle (this cost is included in PR 3) and balance the previous deviation and underspending. Overall, these are timing/efficiency issues and don’t affect project implementation.

fr.name: Strategy for the Sustainable Management of Mediterranean Forests
fr.description:
StrategyMedFor (SMF) a réuni 7 PP dans 6 pays de la zone de coop. Interreg (dont 2 pays IPA) ainsi que des AP pertinents de la scène forestière EU & internat., pour soutenir la mission 2 du progr., dans le but de protéger, restaurer & valoriser l'environ. & le patrimoine naturel. Ce projet traite particulièrement de l'écosyst. forestier médit.
En portant assistance aux strat. & politiques EU & int. sur les forêts, nous abordons les besoins & lacunes de connaissances en ce qui concerne les stratégies & plans d'action pour gestion durable (GD) des forêts Médit. Pour cette raison, l'objectif principal de SMF est le développement d'une Stratégie de GD des Forêts Méditerranéennes (SSMMF) ciblée sur chaque type de forêt & adaptée aux scénarios climatiques prévisibles. La nouveauté dans le travail est l'intégration de nouvelles tech & de données médit. de grenade échelle dans un contexte spatial, tout en combinant données géospatiales, télédétection, GIS & modélisation climatique dans le développement de la Stratégie. Le changement que nous envisageons est le suivant: notre SSMMF sera un document de référence pour les 10 prochaines années sur la scène EU & médit., et fournira des recommandations scientifiques en ce qui concerne la protection, restauration & gestion en résilience des forêts. Des outils spécifiques sont prévus, comme une cartographie thématique sur l'état des forêts & zones d'action prioritaires, pour rendre la GD plus efficace à l'échelle régionale, nationale & locale.
Nous nous engageons à mettre en œuvre quelques uns des résultats du projet SMF en faisant participer des partenaires opérationnels dans des zones de test clés dans les pays (Médit. orientale) où nous avons identifié des investissements peu nombreux dans la recherche &/ou protection des forêts médit., un transfert de connaissances & consolidation croisée des collab. avec la région médit. (Afrique & Moyen-Orient).
Nos résultats seront transmis à la communauté Interreg à travers le TCP & IDP

The objective of this WP is to develop a Strategy for the sustainable management of Mediterranean Forests and define a set of guidelines, criteria and indicators towards Mediterranean forests protection, restoration and resilience.

Develop geospatial data on the status of and impacts on forests and identify priority areas for local to regional actions to guide protection, restoration and management in support of the Strategy for Sustainable Management of Mediterranean Forests.
Activities to develop on 3 test pilot areas (BiH, GR and MK) (1) to validate SSMMF and maps, (2) design and implement specific action plans and (3) extension of IBP
Capitalization of knowledge & experiences delivered in WP1, WP2 & WP3 supporting HEV forest protection, restoration & management for resilience, by fostering knowledge reuse and multilateral dialogue in target areas and in the Mediterranean basin.

en.name: TACTICAL URBANISM - new innovative solutions for sustainable mobility in the cities to mitigate negative environmental impacts in urban life and make cities more liveable places.
en.description:
The project address common territorial challenges of 1) high rate of car ownership, traffic and road danger 2) spaces for green and for community activities are getting rare in quantity, and/or in quality. To achieve a behavioural change towards sustainable forms of urban mobility and greening the streets people need to understand its consequences and be ready to abandon their cars. Local authorities in small-medium towns don’t have the competencies and tools to raise awareness and influence behaviour.
Objective: To enable the public sector and related entities to effectively involve citizens and increase their commitment to jointly address mobility and public spaces problems in functional urban areas. The expected change is the more active participation of citizens in such interventions.
To meet its objectives, the project takes existing knowledge to design a tactical urbanism methodology (O1.1) which will be used by partners to develop territorial roll-up plans (O2.1), which will describe the long-term plans and demonstration actions. Using the lessons from pilots, the partnership develops and disseminates solutions (O4.1) tailored to the target group. To scale up project results partners develop and recommend specific interventions as well as policy proposals with a signing letter of commitment (O4.2). 
Approach: The project will borrow and adapt the general concept and good practices of tactical urbanism, use them to enable partners to implement solutions, and test the method by implementing pilots. It relies on experience from knowledge providers/municipalities from various countries of the region, thus the transnational nature of the partnership allows the testing of approaches in different settings.
Innovative aspects: Using the principles of tactical urbanism; relying on new participatory tools; using new generation communication tools/digital solutions; applying principles of design thinking; using learning-by-doing principle; people-centred approach.
en.expected_achievements:
Craft a tactical urbanism vision/strategy with clear goals and targets to build new knowledge between urban (mobility) planners and cooperation among relevant actors to better respond to climate change with the involvement of the citizens. 


Increase the level of commitment of citizens to improve innovative city-level urban environmental management long-term plans (7).
Experiment with innovative approaches capable (7) to tackle and improve the situation in the field of sustainable mobility and green living areas and develop the green transition of the cities to mitigate climate changes.
Elaboration of at innovative solutions supporting entities and citizens to change the behaviour (1) and local administration to improve policy instruments (1) that address local governance challenges.

fr.name: URBANISME TACTIQUE - nouvelles solutions innovantes pour une mobilité durable dans les villes afin d'atténuer les impacts environnementaux négatifs de la vie urbaine et de les rendre plus vivables
fr.description:
Le projet répond à des défis territoriaux communs: 1)taux élevé de motorisation, trafic et dangers de la route; 2)espaces verts et les activités communautaires qui se raréfient en quantité et/ou en qualité. Pour parvenir à un changement de comportement en faveur de formes durables de mobilité urbaine et de l'écologisation des rues, les gens doivent en comprendre les conséquences et être prêts à abandonner leur voiture. Les autorités locales des villes petites et moyennes n’ont pas les compétences et outils nécessaires pour sensibiliser et influencer les comportements.
Objectif: Permettre au secteur public et aux entités connexes d'impliquer efficacement les citoyens et d'accroître leur engagement à résoudre conjointement les problèmes de mobilité et d'espaces publics dans les zones urbaines fonctionnelles. Le changement attendu est une participation plus active des citoyens à ces interventions. Pour atteindre ses objectifs, le projet utilisera les connaissances existantes pour concevoir une méthodologie d'urbanisme tactique (O1.1) qui sera utilisée par les partenaires pour développer des plans de déploiement territorial (O2.1), qui décriront les plans à long terme et les actions pilotes de démonstration. En utilisant les leçons tirées des actions pilotes, le partenariat développera et diffusera des solutions (O4.1) adaptées au groupe cible. Pour renforcer les résultats du projet, les partenaires élaboreront et recommanderont des interventions spécifiques ainsi que des propositions de politiques accompagnées d'une lettre d'engagement (O4.2).
Approche: Le projet empruntera et adaptera le concept général et les bonnes pratiques de l'urbanisme tactique, les utilisera pour permettre aux partenaires de mettre en œuvre des solutions et testera la méthode en mettant en œuvre des projets pilotes. Il s'appuie sur l'expérience de fournisseurs de connaissances/municipalités de différents pays de la région. La nature transnationale du partenariat permet donc de tester des approches dans différents contextes.
Aspects innovants: Utilisation des principes de l'urbanisme tactique; recours à de nouveaux outils participatifs; utilisation d'outils de communication et de solutions numériques de nouvelle génération; application des principes de la pensée conceptuelle; utilisation du principe de l'apprentissage par la pratique; approche centrée sur les personnes.

en.name: Allocated Zones for circular Aquaculture to trigger the transition to an Inclusive and Circular Economy in aquaculture sector fostering new business opportunities and eco-consciousness society
en.description: Did you know that aquaculture represents a great contributor to aquatic food production worldwide? Especially because most fisheries are near or above sustainable exploitation limits. In Euro-MED, fisheries & aquaculture have been key socioeconomic drivers for millennia, providing livelihoods & MED diet’s basis. Aquaculture is still key economic sector for the area, but faces many challenges: lack of strategies for sustainable development & public sector’s relevant low capacity; linear economy practices/business models traditionally used; complicated legal/licensing framework; lack of dialogue between stakeholders; limited access to space. The overarching challenge of sustainable aquaculture is jointly tackled in AZA4ICE, specifically in close-to-coast & inland waters, where it has great potential for local economies & consumption system. AZA4ICE amplifies the transition to Inclusive & Circular Economy in the sector, implementing innovative ecosystem spatial planning approach embedded with circular practices-production systems in close-to-coast & inland waters. It brings together stakeholders of 5-helix to increase their knowledge/skills, exchange experience & collaborate in drafting their vision & Action Plan for sector’s transition; thus filling the lack of dialogue & blunting conflicts of different uses, leading to new business opportunities & eco-consciousness society. AZA4ICE aquaculture spatial planning approach, stakeholder engagement methodology & Action Plans are the main outputs to be used by public authorities, private sector & academia to benefit the whole society. AZA4ICE approach builds on partners’ existing knowledge & engages 5-helix actors increasing their capacity by exchanging cross boarder experiences; thus it critically depends on transnational cooperation. AZA4ICE is pioneer to suggest circular aquaculture spatial planning & test it in close-to-coast & inland waters going beyond existing practice of aquaculture planning in coastal sea areas.
en.expected_achievements:
Support public agencies and businesses to increase circularity and resource efficiency of aquaculture development in close-to-coast and inland waters through validating a common framework for C-AZA incorporating innovative circular production systems
Bring together multi-level & multi-sectoral stakeholders of 5-helix to co-create C-AZA results and co-decide Action Plans’ drafting, to increase their CE capacity and transnational collaboration and networking in the Euro-MED area and beyond.
Support Public Authorities/agencies in the development and adoption of Action Plans leading to the transition to an Inclusive & Circular Economy in the aquaculture sector and pave the way for circular aquaculture business practices and opportunities 
Contribute to Results Amplification Strategy to support better governance in Euro-MED benefitting civil society at large; monitor project carbon footprint; disseminate project achievements to a wider audience with the support of TCP & IDP.
en.actual_achievements:
AZA4ICE progress in the 1st reporting period was satisfactory to the greatest extent, since the planned activities and deliverables were carried out according to the set time plan. The close cooperation and efficient communication between Project Partners (PPs) was effective and led to the successful achievement of project objectives. Under LP coordination, who had a smooth and efficient communication with the project officer, PPs implemented the necessary administrative pre-contracting phase’s activities to ensure the quick finalisation of the contracting procedures and as a result to ensure than no delays will occur in the beginning of the project. Immediately after AZA4ICE approval, PPs, with LP’s guidance, discussed in detail the next steps for project’s implementation and the testing activities; drafting a detailed work plan and the responsibility assignment matrix – RACI. They also formed the Steering Committee and the Technical Committee at the very beginning of the project. AZA4ICE organised the project kick-off meeting with physical presence of the partners just on the 2nd month of project implementation. In this way, partners got known each other better and efficiently planned the activities to be carried out in the 1st reporting period. PPs formed also the Communication Team. The early activation of all the aforementioned committees was proven valuable for the effective management and project coordination. Regarding the communication of the project, PPs worked on the project’s promotional material and operationalisation of the AZA4ICE website and social media, which are being used for the dissemination of the content and objectives of AZA4ICE. The most important achievements were performed in WP1, contributing in project’s Output 1.1; and in WP2 contributing in project’s Output 2.1.
In the frame of WP1 “Experiment the Allocated Zones for Circular Aquaculture (C-AZA) instrument in the pilot areas”, the scientific and technical partners developed the Del 1.1.1 “AZA4ICE methodology”. This methodology is the cornerstone of the project, since it details the steps that PPs will follow to define C-AZA in their pilot sites. The methodology defines the C-AZA common ecosystem approach to circular aquaculture spatial planning embedded with innovative circular production systems and described the techniques for environmental & socio-economic data collection, exchange with stakeholders, modelling-analyses-KPIs, the expected final results, etc. It is a highly transferable methodology and it is also the first project output, namely Output 1.1 “AZA4ICE methodology for circular aquaculture spatial planning embedded with innovative circular production systems”. Output 1.1 was delivered in the first reporting period, much earlier than initially planned, since the partnership identified that in this way there would be more time for the pilot activities ensuring their smooth and successful execution. PPs also developed the templates for the pilot reporting and the evaluation of the pilot results.
In the frame of WP2 “Living Responsible Research Innovation Ecosystems (LiRRIEs) for quintuple-helix engagement”, the Del 2.1.1 “LiRRIE methodology” was delivered. LiRRIE methodology defines a common multi-level and multi-sectoral stakeholders’ engagement process to set up cooperation patterns and learning processes involving quintuple-helix (academia-industry-government-civil society-environment). It also details the necessary steps for LiRRIEs phases’ operationalization, members’ roles (PPs and 5-helix stakeholders) and their cooperation procedures. The LP, expert in such kind of stakeholders’ engagement processes, provided guidance to all PPs in order to start implementing the LiRRIEs in their territories, identifying the content and mapping the potential stakeholders/participants. The delivery of the LiRRIE methodology justifies the achievement of the Output 2.1 “Living Responsible Research Innovation Ecosystems (LiRRIE) methodology”, in the 1st reporting period as initially planned. 
Summarizing, AZA4ICE has already achieved 2 out of the 4 project outputs in the 1st reporting period, delivering two very useful and highly transferrable methodologies/solutions to the Euro-MED community. For both outputs, the transnational cooperation was an important element, based on which the specificities of all areas were taken into account and the knowledge gained in previous projects and initiatives was capitalised.
Also, AZA4ICE PPs provided feedback to the JS and the governance projects under the Innovative Sustainable Economy Mission when requested, facilitating the creation of synergies between the Mission’s projects and beyond with other Missions, highlighting complementarities and potential actions towards common targets. 
Finally, with the aim to calculate the carbon footprint emissions caused by the project activities, AZA4ICE partners kept CO2 records to be integrated in the Programme’s dedicated online tool.
AZA4ICE progress in the 2nd reporting period was satisfactory to a great extent, since the planned activities and deliverables were carried out according to the set time plan. The close cooperation and communication between Project Partners (PPs) were effective and led to the successful achievement of project objectives. Under Lead Partner’s (LP) coordination, who had a smooth and efficient communication with the project officer, PPs carried out the technical activities of this reporting period, as well as the necessary administrative procedures to report the outcomes reached since the beginning of the project.
The most intense work was done under Work Package 1 (WP1) and Work Package 2 (WP2).
In the frame of WP1 “Experiment the Allocated Zones for Circular Aquaculture (C-AZA) instrument in the pilot areas”, the pilot areas started the implementation of the Del 1.1.1 “AZA4ICE methodology” by collecting field and historical data for the space and species suitability analysis, described in the methodology. This methodology is the cornerstone of the project, detailing the steps that PPs will follow to define C-AZA in their pilot sites (aka the techniques for environmental & socio-economic data collection, exchange with stakeholders, modelling-analyses-KPIs, the expected final results). The PPs responsible for the pilots were supported by the scientific and technical partners and - depending on their capacity - they subcontracted external experts to further support them with the data collection and analysis. The exchange between PPs was extremely beneficial for clarifying how some steps in the methodology should be implemented and the lessons learnt in each pilot were transferred to the others.
In the frame of WP2 “Living Responsible Research Innovation Ecosystems (LiRRIEs) for quintuple-helix engagement”, the PPs followed the Del 2.1.1 “LiRRIE methodology”. LiRRIE methodology defines a common multi-level and multi-sectoral stakeholders’ engagement process involving quintuple-helix, detailing LiRRIEs phases’ operationalization. By the end of the 2nd reporting period, the 2 first LiRRIEs phases were concluded, “Phase 1 – Identify and Map” and “Phase 2 – Motivate and Connect”. In Phase 1, PPs identified the current challenges, needs, barriers and opportunities to be tackled in the framework of LiRRIE and drafted the corresponding LiRRIE vision. They also mapped the relevant stakeholders from all economic sectors to be invited as LiRRIE members. In Phase 2, they formed the “LiRRIE forum” of complementary stakeholders of 5-Helix addressing the common problem/opportunity towards the achievement of the LiRRIE vision with clear understanding of LiRRIE leadership, stakeholder’s commitment, necessary activities and resources. The LP provided guidance to all PPs to implement successfully these LiRRIE phases in their territories. Another activity successfully concluded is the analysis of the aquaculture legal/regulatory framework and licensing processes in all participating countries (Act2.3). This report analyzes the regulatory frameworks and licensing processes shaping the aquaculture sector across the Euro-MED region, emphasizing sustainability, innovation and economic development. As part of Act 2.5 “Engaging civil society and consumers”, the LP and the communication leader started exchanging ideas about the AZA4ICE comic book, ensuring that it will be delivered successfully in the next reporting period. 
In the frame of WP3, PPs started conducting desk research for contributing to the development of the guide with circular aquaculture business practices and opportunities (D3.1.1). Also, the LP has started exchanging with the PPs about the common transnational strategy for drafting the AZA4ICE Action Plans (D3.2.1) for which input from the first LiRRIE events is needed.
Moreover, PPs provided feedback to the JS and the governance projects under the Innovative Sustainable Economy (ISE) Mission when requested, facilitating the creation of synergies between the Mission’s projects and beyond with other Missions, highlighting complementarities and potential actions towards common targets. AZA4ICE participated in all ISE events, physical and online, e.g. the ISEC Hub Launch Event and the 2 ISE Community of Practice events, providing the requested input and feedback.
Finally, with the aim to calculate the carbon footprint emissions caused by the project activities, AZA4ICE partners kept CO2 records to be integrated in the Programme’s dedicated online tool. 
In terms of communication and dissemination, PPs kept updated the AZA4ICE website and the social media accounts. Beyond the basic project communication material (branding, poster, roll-up, etc.), new material was designed as a scientific poster, videos presenting the PPs and the pilot areas, podcasts; and the overall video requested by the JS was drafted. AZA4ICE was also present in some external events prompting the project and its outputs reached till now.
AZA4ICE progress during the 3rd reporting period was largely satisfactory, as the planned activities and deliverables were implemented in line with the established timeline. The effective cooperation and communication among the Project Partners (PPs) contributed significantly to the successful achievement of the project’s objectives. Under the coordination of the Lead Partner (LP), who maintained smooth and efficient communication with the Project Officer, the PPs successfully carried out both technical activities and necessary administrative procedures required to report the outcomes achieved during these months. PPs dedicated significant effort in implementing the activities across all four project Work Packages (WPs).
In WP1 “Experiment the Allocated Zones for Circular Aquaculture (C-AZA) instrument in the pilot areas”, the pilot areas continued the AZA4ICE methodology implementation by collecting field and historical data for the space and species suitability analysis, described in the methodology. The PPs responsible for the pilots were supported by the scientific and technical partners, as well as their external experts, in data collection and analysis. Also, the scientific and technical partners, organised a training session during the 5th project meeting in Sibenik, Croatia, to train PPs and their external experts in using the templates which accompany the methodology for the data collection, analysis and results’ reporting and evaluation (A1.2 and A1.3). This training session was extremely beneficial for clarifying how some steps in the methodology should be implemented. In addition, it was a very good opportunity for exchange of challenges and lessons learnt by the PPs in each area.
In WP2 “Living Responsible Research Innovation Ecosystems (LiRRIEs) for quintuple-helix engagement”, the PPs followed the LiRRIE methodology, which defines a common stakeholders’ engagement process involving quintuple-helix. By the end of the 3rd reporting period, “Phase 3 – Frame and Cross-Fertilise” and “Phase 4 – Deepen” were concluded. In Phase 3, PPs organised the 1st LiRRIE event in each pilot with the stakeholders’ participation. PPs fine-tuned the LiRRIE vision in collaboration with LiRRIE members, cross-fertilised their ideas on AZA4ICE methodology and collected data for the space use and the species, necessary for the AZA4ICE pilots. PPs also applied the BLUEfasma circularity self-assessment tool to the SMEs to identify their readiness and willingness to invest in Circular Economy (CE) in aquaculture. In Phase 4, PPs increased LiRRIE members’ knowledge in CE in the aquaculture sector, as well as in the current legal/regulatory context and licensing processes and relevant innovative circular production systems. This phase enhanced LiRRIE members’ relevant capacity which will facilitate later the co-development of the AZA4ICE Action Plans. LiRRIE initiators organised the 2nd LiRRIEs events. Another activity successfully concluded is the identification of the circularity baseline of businesses in the aquaculture sector using the BLUEfasma Circularity Self-Assessment Tool (Del 2.2.1). PPs faced some challenges in this task, since not all SMEs of the sector felt confident in using the tool by themselves. Due to this, PPs had to put more effort to guide them step-by-step filling the tool. As part of Act 2.5 “Engaging civil society and consumers”, the LP and the communication leader worked on the content creation and the design of the AZA4ICE comic book (D2.5.2), delivering it at the end of this period. They also started exchanging ideas about the AZA4ICE board game (D2.5.1).
In WP3, PPs continued the desk research for the development of the guide with circular aquaculture business practices and opportunities (D3.1.1). Also, the common transnational strategy for drafting the AZA4ICE Action Plans (D3.2.1) was developed and agreed among all PPs.
Moreover, PPs provided feedback to JS and the ISE Mission when requested, facilitating the creation of synergies between projects, highlighting complementarities and potential actions towards common targets. AZA4ICE participated in all ISE events, physical and online, e.g. the 3rd ISE Community of Practice. Finally, PPs kept CO2 records and integrated them in the dedicated online tool. 
In terms of communication and dissemination, PPs kept updated the AZA4ICE website and the social media accounts. Beyond the basic project communication material, new material was designed e.g. dedicated brochure per pilot area and podcasts. AZA4ICE was presented through the interview “Hands in the water, eyes on the future”, conducted with one member of the Portuguese LiRRIE. The interview was conducted as a collaboration action between Interreg Euro-MED and Interreg Atlantic Area and was published at the official interreg.eu website. AZA4ICE was present in external events (e.g. EMD2025, 10th EUSAIR Forum, Circle the MED Forum 8th ed.) reaching a wide audience, promoting the project outputs.

fr.name: Allocated Zones for circular Aquaculture to trigger the transition to an Inclusive and Circular Economy in aquaculture sector fostering new business opportunities and eco-consciousness society
fr.description: Saviez-vous que l'aquaculture contribue grandement à la production alimentaire aquatique dans le monde entier ? D'autant plus que la plupart des pêcheries sont proches ou au-dessus des limites d'exploitation durable. Dans la région Euro-MED, la pêche et l'aquaculture ont été des moteurs socio-économiques clés pendant des millénaires, fournissant les moyens de subsistance et la base du régime alimentaire méditerranéen. L'aquaculture reste un secteur économique clé pour la région, mais elle est confrontée à de nombreux défis : absence de stratégies de développement durable et faible capacité du secteur public en la matière ; pratiques économiques linéaires/modèles d'entreprise traditionnellement utilisés ; cadre juridique/de réglementation complexe ; manque de dialogue entre les parties prenantes ; accès limité à l'espace. Le défi global de l'aquaculture durable est abordé conjointement dans le cadre d'AZA4ICE, en particulier dans les eaux côtières et intérieures, où elle a un grand potentiel pour les économies locales et le système de consommation. AZA4ICE amplifie la transition vers une économie inclusive et circulaire dans le secteur, en mettant en œuvre une approche innovante de planification spatiale de l'écosystème intégrée avec des pratiques circulaires et des systèmes de production dans les eaux intérieures et littorales. Il rassemble les parties prenantes des 5-hélices pour améliorer leurs connaissances/compétences, échanger sur leurs expériences et collaborer à l'élaboration de leur vision et de leur plan d'action pour la transition du secteur, comblant ainsi le manque de dialogue et atténuant les conflits entre les différentes utilisations, conduisant à de nouvelles opportunités d'affaires et à une société éco-consciente. L'approche de planification spatiale de l'aquaculture d'AZA4ICE, la méthodologie d'engagement des parties prenantes et les plans d'action sont les principales réalisations qui seront utilisées par les autorités publiques, le secteur privé et le monde universitaire au profit de l'ensemble de la société. L'approche d'AZA4ICE s'appuie sur les connaissances existantes des partenaires et engage les acteurs des 5-hélices en augmentant leur capacité par l'échange d'expériences transfrontalières ; elle dépend donc de manière critique de la coopération transnationale. AZA4ICE est pionnier pour proposer une approche circulaire des aménagements aquacoles et la teste dans des eaux intérieures et proches de la côte, allant au-delà des pratiques existantes de planification de l'aquaculture dans les zones maritimes côtières.
